D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: Let any two full ring-spans be drawn through the center mark, each terminated in both directions by the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: A full ring-span is defined as a true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated at the ring-path.

proof_steps.claim: Each full ring-span is composed of two true traces whose extremities are the center mark and the marks on the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: Because the full ring-span is drawn through the center mark and terminated by the ring-path, the center mark divides the full ring-span into two true traces.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: Let any two full ring-spans be drawn through the center mark, each terminated in both directions by the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: A full ring-span is defined as a true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated at the ring-path.

proof_steps.claim: Each full ring-span is composed of two true traces whose extremities are the center mark and the marks on the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: Because the full ring-span is drawn through the center mark and terminated by the ring-path, the center mark divides the full ring-span into two true traces.

proof_steps.claim: The two true traces comprising the first full ring-span are respectively alike to the two true traces comprising the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: Since all true traces falling upon the ring from the center mark are alike to one another, any two such traces are alike to any other two.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike. Since the component true traces from the center mark are alike, their sums (the full ring-spans) must be alike.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: Let any two full ring-spans be drawn through the center mark, each terminated in both directions by the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: A full ring-span is defined as a true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated at the ring-path.

proof_steps.claim: Each full ring-span is composed of two true traces whose extremities are the center mark and the marks on the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: Because the full ring-span is drawn through the center mark and terminated by the ring-path, the center mark divides the full ring-span into two true traces.

proof_steps.claim: The two true traces comprising the first full ring-span are respectively alike to the two true traces comprising the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: Since all true traces falling upon the ring from the center mark are alike to one another, any two such traces are alike to any other two.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike. Since the component true traces from the center mark are alike, their sums (the full ring-spans) must be alike.

Step 1 correctly establishes the property of traces from the center mark (radii) being alike using D15 and D16. Step 2 introduces the full ring-spans (diameters) using D17. Step 3 accurately decomposes the full ring-spans into two true traces originating from the center mark, which is geometrically valid under D03 and D17. Step 4 correctly identifies that these component traces are alike to one another, applying D15 and CN1. Finally, Step 5 correctly applies Common Notion 2 (CN2) to assert that the sums of these alike components (the full ring-spans) must also be alike. The terminology is consistent with the provided formal system and there are no logical gaps.

T002.author: A2
T002.claim: If a true trace falls on two co-running true traces, the inside spreads on the same side are not less than two square spreads.

proof_steps.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of co-running true traces.

proof_steps.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the postulate concerning inside spreads and meeting true traces.

proof_steps.claim: If a true trace falling on two co-running true traces made the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the co-running true traces would meet on that side.
proof_steps.justification: This follows by applying the condition of the postulate to the specific case of co-running true traces.

proof_steps.claim: Therefore, the inside spreads on the same side cannot be less than two square spreads, because co-running true traces do not meet in either direction.
proof_steps.justification: If the inside spreads were less than two square spreads, the traces would meet, which directly contradicts the definition of co-running true traces.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: Let any two full ring-spans be drawn through the center mark, each terminated in both directions by the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: A full ring-span is defined as a true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated at the ring-path.

proof_steps.claim: Each full ring-span is composed of two true traces whose extremities are the center mark and the marks on the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: Because the full ring-span is drawn through the center mark and terminated by the ring-path, the center mark divides the full ring-span into two true traces.

proof_steps.claim: The two true traces comprising the first full ring-span are respectively alike to the two true traces comprising the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: Since all true traces falling upon the ring from the center mark are alike to one another, any two such traces are alike to any other two.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike. Since the component true traces from the center mark are alike, their sums (the full ring-spans) must be alike.

Step 1 correctly establishes the property of traces from the center mark (radii) being alike using D15 and D16. Step 2 introduces the full ring-spans (diameters) using D17. Step 3 accurately decomposes the full ring-spans into two true traces originating from the center mark, which is geometrically valid under D03 and D17. Step 4 correctly identifies that these component traces are alike to one another, applying D15 and CN1. Finally, Step 5 correctly applies Common Notion 2 (CN2) to assert that the sums of these alike components (the full ring-spans) must also be alike. The terminology is consistent with the provided formal system and there are no logical gaps.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: Let any two full ring-spans be drawn through the center mark, each terminated in both directions by the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: A full ring-span is defined as a true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated at the ring-path.

proof_steps.claim: Each full ring-span is composed of two true traces whose extremities are the center mark and the marks on the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: Because the full ring-span is drawn through the center mark and terminated by the ring-path, the center mark divides the full ring-span into two true traces.

proof_steps.claim: The two true traces comprising the first full ring-span are respectively alike to the two true traces comprising the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: Since all true traces falling upon the ring from the center mark are alike to one another, any two such traces are alike to any other two.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike. Since the component true traces from the center mark are alike, their sums (the full ring-spans) must be alike.

Finally, Step 5 correctly applies Common Notion 2 (CN2) to assert that the sums of these alike components (the full ring-spans) must also be alike. The terminology is consistent with the provided formal system and there are no logical gaps.

T002.author: A2
T002.claim: If a true trace falls on two co-running true traces, the inside spreads on the same side are not less than two square spreads.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: The two true traces comprising the first full ring-span are respectively alike to the two true traces comprising the second full ring-span.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike. Since the component true traces from the center mark are alike, their sums (the full ring-spans) must be alike.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: Let any two full ring-spans be drawn through the center mark, each terminated in both directions by the ring-path.

proof_steps.claim: The two true traces comprising the first full ring-span are respectively alike to the two true traces comprising the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: Since all true traces falling upon the ring from the center mark are alike to one another, any two such traces are alike to any other two.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike. Since the component true traces from the center mark are alike, their sums (the full ring-spans) must be alike.

Step 4 correctly identifies that these component traces are alike to one another, applying D15 and CN1. The terminology is consistent with the provided formal system and there are no logical gaps.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: Let any two full ring-spans be drawn through the center mark, each terminated in both directions by the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: A full ring-span is defined as a true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated at the ring-path.

proof_steps.claim: Each full ring-span is composed of two true traces whose extremities are the center mark and the marks on the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: Because the full ring-span is drawn through the center mark and terminated by the ring-path, the center mark divides the full ring-span into two true traces.

proof_steps.claim: The two true traces comprising the first full ring-span are respectively alike to the two true traces comprising the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: Since all true traces falling upon the ring from the center mark are alike to one another, any two such traces are alike to any other two.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike. Since the component true traces from the center mark are alike, their sums (the full ring-spans) must be alike.

Step 4 correctly identifies that these component traces are alike to one another, applying D15 and CN1. Finally, Step 5 correctly applies Common Notion 2 (CN2) to assert that the sums of these alike components (the full ring-spans) must also be alike. The terminology is consistent with the provided formal system and there are no logical gaps.

T002.author: A2
T002.claim: If a true trace falls on two co-running true traces, the inside spreads on the same side are not less than two square spreads.

proof_steps.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of co-running true traces.

proof_steps.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the postulate concerning inside spreads and meeting true traces.

proof_steps.claim: If a true trace falling on two co-running true traces made the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the co-running true traces would meet on that side.
proof_steps.justification: This follows by applying the condition of the postulate to the specific case of co-running true traces.

proof_steps.claim: Therefore, the inside spreads on the same side cannot be less than two square spreads, because co-running true traces do not meet in either direction.
proof_steps.justification: If the inside spreads were less than two square spreads, the traces would meet, which directly contradicts the definition of co-running true traces.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.claim: Let any two full ring-spans be drawn through the center mark, each terminated in both directions by the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: A full ring-span is defined as a true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated at the ring-path.

proof_steps.claim: Each full ring-span is composed of two true traces whose extremities are the center mark and the marks on the ring-path.
proof_steps.justification: Because the full ring-span is drawn through the center mark and terminated by the ring-path, the center mark divides the full ring-span into two true traces.

proof_steps.claim: The two true traces comprising the first full ring-span are respectively alike to the two true traces comprising the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: Since all true traces falling upon the ring from the center mark are alike to one another, any two such traces are alike to any other two.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
proof_steps.justification: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike. Since the component true traces from the center mark are alike, their sums (the full ring-spans) must be alike.

Step 4 correctly identifies that these component traces are alike to one another, applying D15 and CN1. Finally, Step 5 correctly applies Common Notion 2 (CN2) to assert that the sums of these alike components (the full ring-spans) must also be alike. The terminology is consistent with the provided formal system and there are no logical gaps.

D01.claim: A mark is that which has no part.
D01.author: SYSTEM

D03.claim: The extremities of a trace are marks.
D03.author: SYSTEM

D04.claim: A true trace is a trace which lies evenly with the marks on itself.
D04.author: SYSTEM

D05.claim: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
D05.author: SYSTEM

D06.claim: The extremities of a surface are traces.
D06.author: SYSTEM

D07.claim: A flat surface, or field, is a surface which lies evenly with the true traces on itself.
D07.author: SYSTEM

D08.claim: A field spread is the inclination to one another of two traces in a field which meet one another and do not lie in a true trace.
D08.author: SYSTEM

D09.claim: When the traces containing the spread are true, the spread is called a true-trace spread.
D09.author: SYSTEM

D10.claim: When a true trace standing on a true trace makes the neighboring spreads alike to one another, each of the alike spreads is a square spread, and the true trace standing on the other is called cross-standing to that on which it stands.
D10.author: SYSTEM

D11.claim: A wide spread is a spread greater than a square spread.
D11.author: SYSTEM

D12.claim: A narrow spread is a spread less than a square spread.
D12.author: SYSTEM

D13.claim: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
D13.author: SYSTEM

D14.claim: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
D14.author: SYSTEM

D15.claim: A ring is a field figure contained by one trace such that all the true traces falling upon it from one mark among those lying within the figure are alike to one another.
D15.author: SYSTEM

D16.claim: And that mark is called the center mark of the ring.
D16.author: SYSTEM

D17.claim: A full ring-span is any true trace drawn through the center mark and terminated in both directions by the ring-path, and such a true trace also halves the ring.
D17.author: SYSTEM

D18.claim: A half-ring is the figure contained by the full ring-span and the ring-path cut off by it.
D18.author: SYSTEM

D19.claim: True-trace figures are those contained by true traces: triads being those contained by three, four-trace figures by four, and many-trace figures by more than four true traces.
D19.author: SYSTEM

D20.claim: Of triads, an all-alike-span triad has its three spans alike, a two-alike-span triad has two of its spans alone alike, and a no-alike-span triad has its three spans all unalike.
D20.author: SYSTEM

D21.claim: Further, of triads: a square-spread triad has a square spread, a wide-spread triad has a wide spread, and a narrow-spread triad has three narrow spreads.
D21.author: SYSTEM

D22.claim: Of four-trace figures: an alike-span square-spread figure is both all-alike-span and all-square-spread; a long figure is square-spread but not alike-span; a tilt-alike figure is alike-span but not square-spread; and a tilt-long figure has its opposite spans and spreads alike to one another but is neither alike-span nor square-spread.
D22.author: SYSTEM

D23.claim: Co-running true traces are true traces which, being in the same field and being extended indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
D23.author: SYSTEM

P1.claim: A true trace can be drawn from any mark to any mark.
P1.author: SYSTEM

P2.claim: A finite true trace can be extended continuously in a true trace.
P2.author: SYSTEM

P3.claim: A ring can be described with any center mark and any gap.
P3.author: SYSTEM

P4.claim: All square spreads are alike to one another.
P4.author: SYSTEM

P5.claim: If a true trace falling on two true traces makes the inside spreads on the same side less than two square spreads, the two true traces, if extended indefinitely, meet on that side.
P5.author: SYSTEM

CN1.claim: Things which are alike to the same thing are alike to one another.
CN1.author: SYSTEM

CN2.claim: If alikes are added to alikes, the wholes are alike.
CN2.author: SYSTEM

CN3.claim: If alikes are subtracted from alikes, the remainders are alike.
CN3.author: SYSTEM

CN4.claim: Things which coincide with one another are alike to one another.
CN4.author: SYSTEM

CN5.claim: The whole is greater than the part.
CN5.author: SYSTEM

T001.author: A1
T001.claim: In any ring, all full ring-spans are alike to one another.

proof_steps.claim: Let a ring be given with a center mark; all true traces falling upon the ring from this center mark are alike to one another.
proof_steps.justification: This is established by the definition of a ring and its center mark.

proof_steps.justification: Since all true traces falling upon the ring from the center mark are alike to one another, any two such traces are alike to any other two.

proof_steps.claim: The whole of the first full ring-span is alike to the whole of the second full ring-span.
